title: Event 1
content:
Summary Report: “FCAI Workshop” Event Objectives Share effective and cost-efficient methods and tools for learning and practicing AWS services, especially for beginners. Inspire students by sharing practical experiences from Hackathon competitions, encouraging them to step outside their comfort zones. Present a real-world case study on developing an AI-powered SaaS application and emphasize the importance of the DevOps mindset in teamwork. Address common psychological barriers faced by students and young professionals, including overcoming Imposter Syndrome, building self-confidence, and breaking the habit of procrastination. Speakers Huynh Thai Linh – Student The Bowler Team (Huynh An Khuong, Mai Quoc Anh, Nguyen Tran Minh Quan) – FPT University Students Nguyen Thi Quynh Nhu – Final-year Student, HUTECH University Tran Nghia – Swinburne Alumnus / AI & Machine Learning Developer (Founder of the Dai Viet Astrology Project) Tran Minh Quan – FPT University Student / Freelance Web Application Developer Pham Phat Uy – Vietnam German University (VGU) Student Key Highlights Cost-Effective AWS Learning Strategy Combine AWS Cloud Quest (an interactive 3D learning game) to understand cloud architecture concepts with Floci or LocalStack (AWS service emulators) to test applications locally before deploying them to the real AWS cloud, significantly reducing costs. Understanding the Value of Hackathons Hackathons are not just coding competitions; they are opportunities to develop resilience under pressure (such as coding continuously for 36 hours during the VNG Lotus Hackathon), expand professional networks, and strengthen a resume. The key lesson is: Do not worry about lacking experience. Participate, learn by doing, and always prepare a product demonstration. Case Study: “Đại Việt Astrology” AI-Powered SaaS Project Transform traditional astrology knowledge into a modern web application. Address AI hallucination problems by combining deterministic rule-based logic (knowledge extracted from books) with a Vector Database (RAG) before sending requests to the Claude 3.5 Sonnet language model. The Hidden Iceberg of DevOps Coding errors and deployment failures are only the visible part of a project’s problems. The hidden causes of failure include unclear requirements, poor communication, manual processes, and a lack of ownership. Four core DevOps principles: Collaboration Automation Fast Feedback Continuous Improvement Applied Psychology: Confidence and Procrastination Confidence: Understanding Imposter Syndrome and the Dunning-Kruger Effect, especially the “Valley of Despair” encountered while learning new skills. Procrastination: Procrastination is not caused by laziness but by psychological barriers such as fear of failure and fear of judgment. The brain naturally seeks short-term comfort to avoid stressful situations. Key Takeaways Mindset & Psychology Action creates confidence: Confidence is the result of taking action, not a prerequisite for getting started. Waiting for perfection only delays progress. Understanding procrastination: Learning how the conflict between the prefrontal cortex (long-term planning) and the limbic system (short-term emotions) influences behavior helped me realize that procrastination is a psychological challenge rather than a sign of laziness. Technology & Architecture Local AWS Environment: Learned how to use Docker, Boto3, Terraform, and Floci to simulate and test more than 50 AWS services locally before deploying them to the cloud. Reliable AI Architecture: Understood the complete AI data processing pipeline: Input → Post Parser → Vector Search (RAG) → LLM → Validation, which significantly improves response accuracy compared to relying solely on prompts. Teamwork & Personal Development Technical tools such as CI/CD and Docker are important, but people, communication, and the DevOps mindset ultimately determine project success. Learned practical productivity techniques, including: The 5-Second Rule for making immediate decisions. The 5-Minute Rule for overcoming resistance and starting difficult tasks. Applying to Work Optimize AWS Learning Costs: Install Floci or LocalStack immediately. From now on, all AWS labs will be developed and tested locally before deployment to a real AWS account, ensuring scripts work correctly while minimizing cloud expenses. Expand Networking and Practical Experience: Plan to join a team and participate in at least one Hackathon this year to gain hands-on experience and strengthen my portfolio. Improve Team Collaboration: Apply the DevOps mindset by clarifying requirements and maintaining continuous communication among developers, business analysts, and QA engineers to reduce misunderstandings and rework. Self-Management: Begin using the 5-Minute Rule whenever facing challenging tasks or feeling tempted to procrastinate by browsing social media. Event Experience Participating in the “FCAI Workshop” was both inspiring and relatable because the speakers were current students and alumni who openly shared the same challenges that I am experiencing today. Several moments left a strong impression on me.
Learning from Real Failures I was particularly impressed by the story of accidentally leaving AWS resources running, resulting in an unexpected USD 70 cloud bill. Hearing this real experience helped me understand common mistakes beginners should avoid when learning cloud computing. Listening to The Bowler team’s experience of coding continuously for 36 hours during a Hackathon inspired me with their passion, determination, and willingness to challenge themselves. Exposure to Real-World Technology I was fascinated by the Đại Việt Astrology project. Seeing a traditional cultural topic implemented using modern technologies such as Next.js, Supabase, Large Language Models (LLMs), Vector Databases, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) demonstrated how creatively technology can be applied across different domains. A Wake-Up Call About Psychology The presentation on the “Iceberg of Procrastination” felt as though it was describing my own experiences. Instead of offering generic motivational advice, the speaker explained procrastination from a neuroscience perspective, helping me understand why I tend to delay important tasks and how I can overcome this behavior. Networking and Communication I realized the importance of being proactive and visible. Asking questions and communicating with others is not only a way to solve problems but also the first step toward building professional networks and creating future career opportunities. Key Takeaways At university, you pay to make mistakes; at work, you are paid to avoid mistakes. Therefore, students should take full advantage of their academic years and personal projects to experiment, learn, and fail without fear. AI is an amplifier, not a replacement for understanding. If your own knowledge is weak, AI-generated results will also be unreliable. AI should support learning rather than replace critical thinking. Do not let fear—whether fear of failure, criticism, or making mistakes—prevent you from moving forward. Start building, keep improving, and learn through action. Some event photos Event picture

title: Event 2
content:
Summary Report: “FCAJ Community Day: Data Driven, AI Risen” Event Objectives Share real-world perspectives and enterprise use cases on applying Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Cloud technologies. Update participants on the latest trends in AI Agents (Voice AI, DevOps AI, Amazon Q) and how AI is reshaping the IT job market. Introduce architectural and technical solutions, from choosing between Single-Agent and Multi-Agent architectures to implementing secure connectivity for enterprise AI systems. Speakers Steve Tran – CTO/Founder, Cloud Thinker Danh Hieu Nghi – AI Engineer, Renova Cloud Tran Tuan Kiet – AI Engineer, AWS Student Builder Group Vu Quoc Trung – CEO, Revve AI Phan Thi Bao & Nguyen Dinh Nguyen – Cloud Engineers, Cloud Kinetics Tran Nhat Truong (Wayne) & Dang Thi Minh Anh – Solution Sales, Noventiq Nguyen Tan Toan – AWS Security Builder Key Highlights Career Transformation and AI Solutions for Cloud Operations (Agentic Platform) AI is significantly accelerating software development but is also creating bottlenecks in quality assurance (QA/QC) and infrastructure operations. Cloud Thinker introduced AI Agent solutions that automate incident management, infrastructure code review, FinOps, and security testing. Single-Agent vs. Multi-Agent: A well-designed Single-Agent system can solve approximately 95% of common use cases. Multi-Agent architectures are more complex but become necessary for implementing Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and overcoming context window limitations. Building Voice AI Agents Compared two architectures: Direct Speech-to-Speech Three-stage pipeline: Speech-to-Text (STT) → Large Language Model (LLM) → Text-to-Speech (TTS) For Vietnamese, a low-resource language, the three-stage architecture provides better content control and more accurate tool calling for banking and enterprise applications. Practical challenges include reducing latency through streaming, recognizing speaker gender for appropriate pronouns, and handling interruptions during conversations. AWS DevOps AI Agent for Automated System Troubleshooting Addressed the issue of fragmented telemetry, where scattered logs make troubleshooting inefficient. Introduced a four-stage AI workflow: Triage Investigation Mitigation Improvement Emphasized an important safety principle: AI should only provide recommendations, while humans remain responsible for approving and executing actions. Amazon Q Business for Human Resources Demonstrated how Amazon Q addresses the inefficiencies of manual resume screening, reduces the risk of overlooking qualified candidates, and minimizes subjective evaluation. Amazon Q can automatically generate job descriptions (JDs), extract information from resumes, score candidates against job requirements, and produce comparison reports quickly while maintaining data security. Securing Connectivity Between Amazon Q and MCP Servers Enterprise AI systems should not expose public endpoints due to risks such as Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks and Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks. The recommended solution is to deploy the MCP Server inside a Private Subnet, using VPC Interface Endpoints, Route 53 Resolver, and an Application Load Balancer (ALB) to establish secure private communication with Amazon Q. Key Takeaways Design and Operations Mindset AI Supports Rather Than Replaces Humans: AI should improve productivity rather than replace human decision-making, especially in high-risk production environments. Understand the Fundamentals: Developers should never rely solely on AI-generated outputs. Strong foundational knowledge is essential for validating AI-generated results, particularly in DevOps and Data Engineering. Technical Architecture Observability Is the Foundation: DevOps AI Agents can only operate effectively when systems provide comprehensive logs, metrics, and traces. Voice AI Architecture: Learned the complete streaming workflow from Speech-to-Text through LLM processing to Text-to-Speech, enabling low-latency real-time interactions. Zero Trust Security: Any SaaS service, such as Amazon Q, accessing internal systems should communicate through private infrastructure, including Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs) and Private Subnets. AI Integration Strategy AI applications should mitigate hallucination risks by restricting the Agent’s operational scope and incorporating deterministic rules wherever possible. Applying to Work Enhancing Personal Projects: Instead of building only traditional CRUD applications, I plan to integrate the Model Context Protocol (MCP) so AI can securely interact with databases and external systems. Security-First Design: Apply Private Subnet and VPC networking principles when developing cloud-based academic projects rather than exposing unnecessary public endpoints. Improving My Resume: Optimize my CV using relevant technical keywords because many companies now use AI-powered resume screening before human review. Event Experience Participating in the “FCAJ Community Day” workshop provided me with a completely new perspective on how AI is applied in enterprise environments. The event demonstrated that AI is far more than a chatbot—it has become an essential component of modern software systems and business operations.
Learning from Industry Experts Listening directly to founders, CTOs, and senior engineers helped me recognize the gap between academic knowledge and real-world enterprise requirements, particularly in system operations, cost optimization, and security. Hands-on Technical Experience I was highly impressed by the live demonstration of a Voice AI Agent answering questions about MacBook products entirely through voice interaction. It clearly illustrated the technical challenges of minimizing latency and handling conversational interruptions. Another memorable demonstration simulated a Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attack and showed how a DevOps AI Agent analyzed system logs to identify the actual root cause, providing a realistic view of production-level operations. Modern AI Tools in Practice I was amazed by Amazon Q’s ability to automatically analyze dozens of resumes and generate candidate evaluation reports within seconds, demonstrating the practical value of AI in recruitment. Networking and Discussion The event featured many insightful questions from attendees, helping me realize that while technical knowledge is important, understanding and solving business problems is the ultimate objective of technology. Key Takeaways AI is evolving at an extraordinary pace. Instead of using AI merely to automate traditional tasks, developers should leverage AI to amplify their capabilities and solve more complex problems. Technologies and tools may change rapidly, but architectural principles and security best practices remain fundamental and timeless. Career opportunities are not disappearing; however, employer expectations are increasing significantly. Modern software engineers are expected to possess both advanced technical expertise and practical AI experience. Therefore, students should continuously improve their skills and build high-quality side projects that demonstrate real-world capabilities. Some event photos

title: Event 3
content:
Summary Report: “Amazon Web Services (AWS): Enterprise Cloud Architectures and Industry Applications” Event Objectives Provide students with opportunities to gain real-world insights by learning from AWS experts and engineers who actively use cloud technologies in enterprise environments. Update students on current recruitment trends and practical skill requirements in the IT industry, particularly in Cloud Computing, Data Engineering, and Artificial Intelligence. Bridge the gap between university coursework (such as COS2019 – Cloud Computing Architecture) and real-world industry practices. Inspire students to identify their personal challenges and develop a clear career roadmap for long-term professional growth. Speakers Nguyen Tran Minh Duy – Industry Liaison Officer, Swinburne Vietnam Nguyen Gia Hung – Head of Solutions Architecture in Vietnam & Cambodia, Amazon Web Services (AWS); Founder of FCAJ Banh Cam Vinh – Senior Data Engineer, Renova Cloud Tran Thi Quynh Nhu – Account Manager, Amazon Web Services (AWS) Nguyen Hoang Khang – Solutions Architect, Cloud Kinetics; Swinburne Vietnam Alumnus (K3) Key Highlights IT Job Market and Cloud/AI Trends The cloud job market is expanding rapidly, but expectations have also increased significantly. Even internship positions now often require knowledge of technologies such as Kubernetes. AWS is focusing its investment in Vietnam on three strategic areas: Local Talent Development Infrastructure Expansion (Local Zone) Technical Skill Development (Skilling) AI is reshaping the employment landscape by reducing demand for junior engineers while increasing demand for experienced professionals, creating an inverted talent pyramid. AI-powered recruitment systems also evaluate resumes based on continuous self-improvement and learning activities. Rather than choosing only a technical role, candidates should also specialize in a particular industry (for example, becoming a Data Engineer for the retail industry) to create a stronger professional profile. The Difference Between Academic and Enterprise Data Engineering At university: Clean datasets Clearly defined requirements Flexible schedules Low consequences for mistakes In enterprise environments: Incomplete or noisy data Frequently changing business requirements Tight deadlines Significant business impact when failures occur Companies care about much more than simply moving data into dashboards. Most engineering effort is dedicated to data quality, failure handling, security, and operational reliability. The most important mindset is “One framework for every platform.” Engineers should understand the core architecture of a data platform—including data ingestion, processing, storage, and analytics—because tools evolve, while architectural principles remain stable. Overcoming Fear and Creating Opportunities Many students fear making mistakes or being judged, largely due to traditional educational environments. The best way to overcome these fears is through continuous practice, such as treating every public speaking opportunity as an internship. Miscommunication often occurs because different stakeholders focus on different priorities. For example, sales teams emphasize business value, while engineers focus primarily on technical implementation. Instead of competing in the crowded “Red Ocean” by only submitting resumes through job portals, students should create a “Blue Ocean” by networking, engaging in small talk, and increasing their professional visibility. Mindset for Fresh Graduates in the AI Era The biggest mistake is allowing AI to complete assignments without understanding the underlying concepts, effectively outsourcing one’s own understanding. At university, students pay to make mistakes; in the workplace, professionals are paid to avoid mistakes. Therefore, foundational subjects such as Computer Networks, Data Structures, and Databases remain critically important. Employers generally evaluate candidates based on: Attitude Technical Competence Experience Practical Exposure Personal Qualities Rather than focusing solely on the starting salary, fresh graduates should prioritize gaining experience, expanding their professional network, acquiring knowledge, and achieving continuous personal growth. Key Takeaways Mindset and Professional Development The Success Formula:
Result = Capability × Visibility × Consistency
Being technically skilled is not enough. Professionals must consistently demonstrate their abilities and maintain continuous improvement. Lifelong Learning: Continuously update resumes, develop side projects, and write technical blogs. AI-powered recruitment systems increasingly evaluate these activities when assessing candidates. Attitude Matters More Than Skills: For entry-level positions, employers value initiative, a willingness to learn, and resilience under pressure more than extensive technical experience. Technical Architecture Strong foundational knowledge remains the most valuable asset. AI serves only as an amplifier; weak fundamentals inevitably lead to incorrect outputs. Understanding the “DNA” of software systems, such as data platforms, is more important than mastering individual tools. Engineers should focus on architectural principles rather than chasing technology trends. Personal projects can be significantly improved by using AI tools such as Amazon Q to review code and identify production-level issues, including security vulnerabilities and inadequate error handling. Communication and Career Strategy Understand Business Problems: Software engineers should focus not only on writing code but also on understanding business objectives. Effective communication with different departments is essential for gathering accurate requirements. Embrace Imperfection: Accept the fear of making mistakes and continuously ask “Why?” to develop a deeper understanding of technical and business challenges. The Blue Ocean Strategy: Participate actively in technical communities and professional events, build relationships with industry experts, and pursue opportunities through networking and referrals rather than relying solely on traditional job applications. Applying to Work Improve Personal Projects: Instead of creating generic applications, align projects with a specific industry domain, such as FinTech or Healthcare, while incorporating comprehensive security mechanisms and error-handling strategies. Increase Professional Visibility: Begin writing technical blogs, publishing well-documented GitHub repositories, and consistently sharing learning experiences with the developer community. Communicate More Proactively: Practice small talk and actively ask questions during seminars and university lectures to expand professional networks. Use AI Responsibly: Treat AI as a mentor for explaining complex concepts and reviewing code rather than allowing it to generate solutions without understanding the underlying logic. Event Experience Participating in the workshop “Amazon Web Services (AWS): Enterprise Cloud Architectures and Industry Applications” was a major turning point for me. The professional environment and the honest experiences shared by industry experts challenged many of my previous assumptions about the IT industry and provided a much clearer understanding of real-world expectations.
Understanding the Reality of the Job Market Learning that a single junior position may receive over 1,000 applications, while internship candidates are increasingly expected to understand Kubernetes, made me realize that I must accelerate my learning journey immediately. The statement “Good grades do not necessarily guarantee employment” was particularly eye-opening. Learning from Industry Experts Listening to Banh Cam Vinh explain the differences between clean academic datasets and messy enterprise data helped me understand why mastering foundational subjects is so important. I was especially impressed by Nguyen Hoang Khang’s story about candidates who completed recruitment coding assessments rapidly using AI but were unable to explain their own solutions afterward. This completely changed my perspective on how I should use AI in the future. Overcoming Psychological Barriers Tran Thi Quynh Nhu’s presentation about fear and Imposter Syndrome resonated strongly with me. Hearing professionals from major technology companies openly discuss the same fears gave me confidence that these challenges are normal and can be overcome. Key Takeaways Technology is only a tool; mindset is the true foundation. Rather than constantly chasing the latest technologies or frameworks, students should focus on mastering core subjects such as Computer Networks, Databases, and Data Structures. Never outsource your understanding to AI. AI may complete assignments efficiently, but it cannot replace critical thinking during technical interviews or when solving real production problems. Career opportunities do not appear automatically. Students must continuously invest in learning, build high-quality projects, expand their professional networks, and increase their visibility so that opportunities can naturally find them. Some event photos

title: Week 3 Worklog
content:

Proficient in instance administration (modifying configuration, creating Snapshots, packaging Custom AMIs), troubleshooting lost Key Pairs, and practically deploying a Node.js CRUD application. Applied IAM Policies (JSON) to grant permissions based on the principle of least privilege, tightly controlling regions, instance types, and resource deletion rights. title: Week 6 Worklog
content:

Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Researched the Shared Responsibility Model: Delineating the security boundaries of AWS physical infrastructure (Security of the Cloud) from user applications, configurations, and data (Security in the Cloud).

- Hands-on: Initialized a custom JSON policy ec2-admin-restrict-region implementing a Condition constraint bound to the aws:RequestedRegion variable to permit actions strictly within the Singapore region.
- Created an ec2-admin user assigned AmazonEC2FullAccess while applying an upper boundary limit under Permissions Boundary pointing to the newly created region-restriction policy to test API blocking upon switching to another Region.

https://000030.awsstudygroup.com/vi/

Successfully applied Permissions Boundaries to place strict upper-limit constraints on sub-users, completely eliminating the risk of unauthorized privilege escalation even if the user is attached to wide Administrator policies. Smoothly operated Permission Sets connecting enterprise accounts; mastered automated AWS CLI authentication via the aws configure sso command and safe session logout using aws sso logout. Successfully operated AWS Config in coordination with AWS Security Hub to run automated configuration scans aligned with international standards such as AWS Foundational Security Best Practices and PCI DSS. Familiarized with the Auditor role, interpreting risk graphs (Findings), and practicing the remediation or disabling of misconfigurations to optimize security scores on the overview Dashboard. Realizing Cost-Optimized EC2 ChatOps with Lambda:
Successfully built a serverless automation system: Wrote Python boto3 code on AWS Lambda to accurately detect system tags and execute automated scale-down or scale-up cycles for EC2 virtual machines. Successfully integrated Amazon EventBridge as a scheduled trigger to automatically start and stop servers, helping enterprises cut over 60% of wasted costs from Test/Staging environments. Successfully established a Webhook connection pipeline to push visually color-coded server status notifications (Stopping/Starting) to Slack communication channels in real time

title: Week 8 Worklog
content:

Shifted from traditional multi-table normalized design (RDBMS) to single-table design in DynamoDB by leveraging Sort Key overloading techniques and secondary index structures. Gained a deep understanding of synchronization mechanisms, global replication latency in DynamoDB Global Tables, and fault-tolerant architectural strategies designed to withstand the physical outage of an entire region. Researched Change Data Capture (CDC) solutions and applied defensive programming techniques such as deduplication and idempotent record processing to guarantee exactly-once execution semantics. Explored the roadmap for parsing AWS Cost & Usage Reports (CUR), combining advanced tagging techniques for cost allocation and reclaiming abandoned cloud resources. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Researched Data Lake concepts, analyzing 3 core properties: ingesting all types of raw data, supporting multi-users, and enabling flexible access.

- Researched the operational mechanism of AWS Glue Crawlers to automatically scan file structures, detect schemas, and synchronize metadata into the Glue Data Catalog.
- Explored Amazon Athena’s serverless SQL query solution and the visual reporting mechanism via Amazon QuickSight (Data Source, Dataset, Visual, Analysis, Dashboard).
- Researched automated real-time data streaming pipelines using Kinesis Data Firehose combined with Python Boto3 scripts on Glue Interactive Sessions.
- Explored safe system cleanup procedures (deleting Crawlers, Databases, Notebooks, and emptying S3 buckets) to optimize AWS account costs. 08/06/2026 08/06/2026 https://000035.awsstudygroup.com/vi/ 3 - Researched an overview of Amazon DynamoDB key-value databases, developing single-table design methodologies for ultra-large workloads.

Proposal
content:
In this section, I summarize the contents of the workshop on deploying a real-world warehouse and transportation management system to the AWS cloud platform.
WMS Pro: Comprehensive Warehouse & Transportation Management System A Comprehensive Warehouse & Transportation Management Solution on AWS Cloud 1. Executive Summary WMS Pro is an integrated internal logistics management platform designed to solve operational challenges faced by express delivery services. The system connects three core components: Backend (NestJS), Web Dashboard (Next.js), and Mobile App (Flutter). By leveraging AWS Cloud infrastructure (EC2, RDS, S3), WMS Pro provides real-time shipment monitoring, intelligent fleet management, and automated financial reconciliation with absolute precision.
2. Problem Statement Current Issues
Many small and medium-sized logistics providers face difficulties with:
Managing goods loss due to manual handover processes. Cash-on-Delivery (COD) reconciliation discrepancies between shippers and post offices. Slow and inaccurate address entry. Lack of real-time Key Performance Indicator (KPI) reporting systems for business decision-making. The Solution
WMS Pro adopts a strict 12-step State Machine model to lock down the order lifecycle. The system integrates a 2-tier administrative boundary model (2025 standard), optimizing address entry time by 80%. All data operates on Amazon RDS (PostgreSQL) to guarantee transaction integrity (ACID compliance) and Amazon S3 for storing proof of delivery and incident images.
Benefits and Return on Investment (ROI)
Operational Improvement: Reduces dispatch staffing costs by 30% thanks to order assignment algorithms and virtual parking lots. Financial Transparency: E-wallet system and centralized reconciliation eliminate cash leakage risks. Optimized Cost: Utilizing the AWS Free Tier, infrastructure operational costs are estimated at < $10 USD/month during the initial phase. Expected break-even period is 4-6 months due to savings in manual operation and management expenses. 3. Solution Architecture The system employs a Multi-tier Architecture deployed on AWS to ensure security and scalability.
AWS Services Used
Amazon EC2 (t3.micro): Runs Backend application (NestJS) and Web Frontend (Next.js) with Nginx Reverse Proxy. Amazon RDS (PostgreSQL): Relational database storing waybills, user information, and financial transactions. Amazon S3: Stores incident proof images and Proof of Delivery (POD). AWS IAM: Manages resource access permissions via Role-based access control instead of static Access Keys. Amazon CloudFront (Optional): Accelerates static content delivery for the Web App. 4. Technical Implementation Implementation Phases
Analysis & Design: Finalize order state machine and Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) (April). Core API & Database Development: Develop NestJS Backend connecting to RDS, set up pagination, and JWT security (May). Multi-platform Development: Build Web Dashboard for Admin/Warehouse staff and Flutter Mobile App for Shippers (June). Cloud Migration & Testing: Deploy entire system to AWS, integrate S3, and test real-world operational flows (July). Technical Requirements
Backend: Node.js 20+, NestJS, TypeORM. Frontend: Next.js 14+ (App Router), Tailwind CSS. Mobile: Flutter SDK 3.x, Provider/Riverpod for state management. DevOps: Docker, Nginx, PM2 for service maintenance on EC2. 5. Timeline & Milestones April - May: Complete waybill handling logic and e-wallet financial operations. June: Complete “Virtual Parking Lot” and “2-in-1 Order Processing Station” interfaces. July: Week 1: Set up VPC and RDS on AWS. Week 2: Deploy Backend & Web to EC2, configure S3. Week 3: Build Mobile App, perform E2E (End-to-End) testing. Week 4: Final acceptance and handover. 6. Budget Estimation (AWS Free Tier Focused) Compute (EC2): $0.00 USD (750 hours/month t2.micro/t3.micro). Database (RDS): $0.00 USD (750 hours/month, 20GB storage). Storage (S3): ~$0.10 USD (For initial 5GB image data). Data Transfer: ~$0.50 USD (Based on actual traffic). Total Estimated Cost: < $1.00 USD/month during the first year. 7. Risk Assessment Security Risk: Waybill data leak. Mitigation: Use HTTPS, data masking on public tracking links, and Security Group IP restrictions. Operational Risk: Shipper loses network connectivity while delivering. Mitigation: Mobile App supports local offline storage and syncs when reconnected. Financial Risk: Errors in wallet credit/debit logic. Mitigation: Utilize Database Transactions and Pessimistic Locking. 8. Expected Outcomes A Production-ready Logistics management system. Capability to process 10,000+ waybills/month under Free Tier configuration. Seamless, consistent user experience between Web and Mobile. Clean data foundation ready for future integration of route forecasting AI modules.

title: Self-evaluation
content:
During my internship at Amazon Web Services Vietnam Co., Ltd. from April 17, 2026 to July 30, 2026, I had the opportunity to learn, practice, and apply the knowledge acquired at university to a real-world working environment.
I participated in the Comprehensive Warehouse & Transportation Management System (WMS Pro) project. This is a complex project requiring multi-platform integration across Backend (NestJS), Web Dashboard (Next.js), and Mobile App (Flutter). Through this project, I significantly improved my skills in Full-stack development, system architecture thinking, transactional database management (PostgreSQL), and practical Logistics operation workflows.
Regarding my work ethic, due to the nature of focusing on independent technical research and solution development, I was always proactive in searching for documentation and resolving challenging problems. However, I am also aware of my shortcomings in communication and administrative discipline, which I aim to refine and improve in the future.
To objectively reflect on my internship period, I evaluate myself based on the following criteria:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding of the field, applying knowledge in practice, proficiency with tools, work quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Absorption of new knowledge, quick learning ☐ ✅ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Self-researching, taking on tasks without waiting for instructions ✅ ☐ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing tasks on time, ensuring quality ☐ ✅ ☐ 5 Discipline Adhering to schedules, regulations, and work procedures ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Progressive mindset Willingness to receive feedback and self-improve ☐ ✅ ☐ 7 Communication Clearly presenting ideas and reporting work ☐ ✅ ☐ 8 Teamwork Working effectively with colleagues, participating in teams ☐ ☐ ✅ 9 Professional conduct Respecting colleagues, partners, and the work environment ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-solving skills Identifying problems, proposing solutions, showing creativity ✅ ☐ ☐ 11 Contribution to project/team Work effectiveness, innovative improvements, team recognition ☐ ✅ ☐ 12 Overall General evaluation of the entire internship period ☐ ✅ ☐ Needs Improvement Discipline: Need to practice stricter adherence to working hours and administrative regulations of the organization, building professional working habits in a team environment. Problem-solving mindset: Although possessing strong technical execution capability, I need to expand my perspective from “writing code” to “business solutions” so that built features deliver maximum operational value for end users. Communication skills: Since the internship mainly involved working independently on the project, I realized that I need to learn to connect and communicate more with colleagues. I need to improve my ability to present technical topics simply and clearly, as well as enhance my capability to handle unexpected communication situations in daily work.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Overall Evaluation 1. Working Environment
The working environment at AWS is extremely professional, dynamic, and technology-driven. The modern, quiet workspace creates ideal conditions for me to focus on researching challenging knowledge. Although I mainly worked independently on the project, I could still feel the readiness of everyone around to support whenever I needed to discuss infrastructure or company processes.
2. Support from Mentor / Team Admin
The mentor always explained my questions about internal regulations very clearly and in detail, providing a comfortable space for me to execute the project in my own way. The admin team supported extremely effectively in providing documents and completing administrative procedures, allowing me to fully focus on my professional tasks.
3. Relevance of Work to Academic Major
The WMS Pro project is a perfect combination of theoretical knowledge of warehouse management and Full-stack programming skills. Personally building the system from database design to multi-platform deployment helped me firmly consolidate my academic knowledge and gain a deep understanding of practical Logistics operation workflows.
4. Learning & Skill Development Opportunities
This is the biggest opportunity I received. I learned how to manage a large-scale project by myself, from controlling Version Control to handling complex runtime errors. In particular, the “Cloud-native” mindset and the usage of AWS services opened up a new vision for my career.
5. Company Culture & Team Spirit
Despite working independently, I still experienced the culture of respecting diversity and promoting Ownership here. The serious, result-oriented working spirit, combined with an open willingness to share knowledge, is something I deeply appreciate.
6. Internship Policies / Benefits
The support policies and allowances for interns are very clear and satisfactory. The company creating conditions for me to join internal training sessions on new technologies is a highly valuable privilege.
Additional Questions What were you most satisfied with during your internship? It was the freedom and Ownership in the project. Overcoming technical barriers by myself to complete a large system from scratch gave me tremendous confidence in my own abilities. What do you think the company needs to improve for future interns? Besides the weekly academic exchanges which are already great, the company could organize more non-technical Team Bonding activities (such as coffee catch-ups, team games, etc.). This would help interns from different domains connect better on a personal level, reducing the feeling of working in isolation when pursuing independent projects. If recommending to friends, would you advise them to intern here? Why or why not? Absolutely yes. This is a fantastic environment to cultivate discipline and self-learning skills, making it the best launching pad for anyone looking to work in an international environment. Suggestions & Expectations Do you have any suggestions to improve the internship experience? I think the program could organize weekly “Quick Demo” sessions where interns present their project progress and receive multi-dimensional feedback from more people. Would you like to continue this program in the future? I would love to continue working with real-world projects at AWS. Other feedback (freely share): Thank you FCAJ for creating a challenging and meaningful internship journey that helped me grow significantly in both hard skills and work ethic.
